text: Truth is not discovered but interpreted from a perspective. Even the will to truth must be put on trial: why do we want truth rather than untruth, and why have we never asked?
chapter: Part One: On the Prejudices of Philosophers

text: Most philosophy is autobiography — every great thinker's system reveals which drives won the inner war and called themselves 'reason'. The system's official premises are usually the conclusion in disguise.
There is no 'doer' behind the deed. The grammatical 'I' is a fiction we apply to a bundle of competing drives so we have someone to praise or blame.

Two basic moral types contend in history: master morality, which calls 'good' what is noble, strong, life-affirming; and slave morality, which calls 'good' what is meek, harmless, useful to the weak. Modern Christian-democratic ethics is the latter rebranded as universal.
Genuine philosophers are not labourers who measure what is — they are commanders and legislators who say 'thus it shall be' and create new tables of value. Most professors are not philosophers.
The free spirit is not the rebel who rejects every constraint, but the one who can hold opposing convictions in suspense and still act decisively — strength of will without rigidity of dogma.
Suffering is not an objection to life. The depth of a soul is measured by what it can endure without resentment. To pity the strong for their burdens is to misunderstand them.
Modern democracy, socialism, and Christianity share a single underlying instinct: the herd's resentment of anything higher than itself. Their political differences mask a moral identity.
chapter: Part Five: Natural History of Morals

text: He who fights monsters should see to it that he himself does not become a monster. And when you gaze long into the abyss, the abyss also gazes into you.

Beware that, in casting out your demons, you do not throw away the best of yourself. The drives that produce great cruelty are also the drives that produce great achievement; they need to be commanded, not eliminated.

Europe is at a crossroads: it can either sink into a 'last man' contented mediocrity, or breed a new philosophical type — strict with itself, beyond nationalism, willing to legislate values for centuries to come.
Real nobility is not inherited — it is the readiness to take long responsibility for oneself, to keep faith with one's own values across decades, when nobody is watching and nothing is at stake.
